Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I was going to sit down and focus, but then I'd get distracted or just not feel like it. Most of my studying happened in bursts when I remembered something was due or when the anxiety hit me about falling behind. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't get myself to commit to a schedule. It's weird because I actually wanted to do well, but the follow-through wasn't there.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines rather than any regular schedule. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd realize an assignment was due and I'd scramble. I'm 24 and I've been in school long enough to know better, but somehow I kept falling into the same trap. The anxiety would spike right before something was due, and that's when I'd finally get moving.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a deadline or just this nagging feeling that I was missing something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent, scroll around for a bit, and then log off without really doing much. The panic of potentially missing something important was usually what got me to actually open the platform. It wasn't about wanting to learn in that moment—it was more about damage control.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every day or so. But it never stuck. Life stuff would come up, or I'd just lose momentum, and before I knew it I hadn't logged in for days. I never got to that point where it felt automatic or natural to use the LMS regularly.
Q5: The video lectures were actually pretty helpful for me since I learn better by listening than reading. I could put them on and absorb the information without having to stare at dense text. The discussion boards were okay too because I could see how other students were thinking about the material. When I actually engaged with those resources, I felt like I was getting something out of it.
Q6: The reading materials and supplementary PDFs—I barely touched those. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd just sit in a folder on my computer. Reading long documents just doesn't work for my brain the same way audio does. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came. It felt like too much effort for something I wasn't sure would help me.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection to an assignment or exam, that made it feel worth my time. Also, if something was explained in a way that actually made sense and didn't require me to re-read it five times. I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just going through motions. When activities felt disconnected from what actually mattered for my grade, I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist in my mind. I'd see it listed and think 'that's nice' and then never look at it again. Required activities at least had that external pressure pushing me to do something. With optional stuff, my brain just categorized it as 'not necessary' and moved on. I know that's probably not the best approach for actually learning, but that's how I operated.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download the lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone when I was commuting or doing other stuff. Sometimes I'd look things up on YouTube if the course material wasn't clicking for me. I also talked through concepts with a friend who was in a similar program. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS, just not in ways that would show up anywhere.
Q10: There were times when I'd be thinking about course concepts while doing other things, like making connections in my head or mentally reviewing what I'd heard in a lecture. I'd also sometimes re-listen to downloaded audio multiple times, but the LMS would only show that I accessed it once. My engagement wasn't always visible because a lot of it happened in my head or through offline materials.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or audio file once and then play it repeatedly offline over the next few days. The LMS would show one access, but I might have engaged with that content four or five times. It was just easier to have things saved locally so I could study whenever, without needing to be connected or logged in.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. My anxiety was through the roof, and instead of that pushing me to work harder, it kind of paralyzed me. I'd open the LMS, feel overwhelmed by everything I hadn't done, and then close it. The CBT sessions I started actually helped me recognize that pattern, but by then I was already pretty far behind.
Q13: Definitely. When I had other responsibilities piling up—work stuff, family things—the course was the first thing to slide. Fatigue was a big one too; I'd come home exhausted and the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS. Confusion also played a role because when I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it rather than ask for help. It was easier to just not engage.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd be logged in, maybe have a video playing, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd realize I'd zoned out for like ten minutes and had no idea what was just said. Those sessions technically counted as engagement, but I wasn't actually absorbing anything. It was more like going through the motions.
Q15: The bigger assignments terrified me, so I'd put them off even though I knew they were important. The anxiety about doing them poorly made it easier to just not start. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing. By the time I finally started, I'd run out of time to do quality work. It's a frustrating cycle I'm still trying to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything at all. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly the LMS became very important. But that also meant I was always rushing and never really giving myself time to properly engage with the material. Everything was last-minute and stressful.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but I didn't always check it right away. If the feedback was encouraging, it motivated me a bit. If it pointed out problems, sometimes it made me want to try harder, but other times it just added to my anxiety about the course. The timing mattered too—feedback that came too late felt pointless because I'd already moved on mentally.
Q18: The structure felt kind of loose to me, which wasn't great for someone like me who needs external pressure to stay on track. If there had been more regular check-ins or smaller deadlines throughout, I might have kept up better. The way it was set up, it was too easy for me to drift and then suddenly realize I was way behind.
Q19: Having everything available online was convenient, but it also made it easy to ignore. If I had to show up somewhere physically, I would have engaged more consistently. The discussion boards were nice in theory, but I never felt like my participation really mattered. I think more structure and accountability would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I can totally relate to that. Sometimes you're logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos or reading posts, but you're not actually producing anything. It might be that you're anxious about submitting work that isn't good enough, or you're trying to understand the material before you feel ready to attempt an assignment. High activity doesn't always mean you're in a place to actually complete things.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Some people are just efficient—they know what they need, they get it, and they work offline. Or they have other resources and don't need to rely on the LMS as much. I did some of my better thinking away from the platform, so I can see how someone could perform okay without showing tons of LMS activity.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show whether you actually understood anything or cared about what you were doing. It misses all the offline studying, the mental processing, the conversations with classmates. It also can't tell the difference between someone who's genuinely engaged and someone who's just clicking through anxiously.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it. It's not just about completing tasks—it's about being mentally present and curious. I think I wanted to be engaged, but my anxiety and avoidance patterns got in the way. Real engagement feels like you're investing in yourself, not just checking boxes.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling internally or how much anxiety was affecting my ability to participate. It wouldn't show the times I listened to lectures while walking or the conversations I had with friends about course material. It also wouldn't capture that I actually cared about doing well, even though my actions didn't always reflect that. There's a whole emotional and mental side to my experience that just doesn't translate into clicks and timestamps.
